title: Week 16 scaling ladder
acceptanceTarget: Provide a gradual route from single container + Postgres to Redis workers instead of starting with Kubernetes.
principles: Use queue mode when execution concurrency and worker separation are real bottlenecks
Use separate webhook processors only after webhook ingress becomes the bottleneck
Consider Kubernetes only after queue mode operations, managed DB/Redis, centralized logs, and platform skill are mature

stages.label: local / learning single container
stages.architecture: single n8n container, local volume, no production public ingress
entryCriteria: Learning or demo workflows
No business-critical credentials
No production webhook exposure
requiredSettings: fixed N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY if any credentials matter
exitSignals: Workflow becomes business-critical
Need stable webhook URL
Need durable database backup
doNotSkip: Do not use tunnel as production
Do not store important credentials without backup

stages.label: single container + PostgreSQL
stages.architecture: n8n single container with PostgreSQL durable state
entryCriteria: Workflow stores real business data
Credentials and executions need restore
Single process is still enough for traffic
requiredSettings: PostgreSQL host, database, user, password, schema
exitSignals: p95 execution latency rises under normal production load
Active executions overlap frequently
Memory or CPU high-water marks become routine
doNotSkip: Do not add Redis before PostgreSQL is stable
Do not add workers before restore is proven

stages.label: larger single instance + PostgreSQL + concurrency control
stages.architecture: larger single n8n instance with tuned execution data and concurrency
entryCriteria: CPU or memory pressure is visible but not consistently saturated
Workload can still be handled by one n8n process
Queue backlog is not yet a persistent problem
requiredSettings: central logs for the single instance
exitSignals: Regular mode concurrency queues frequently
Main process becomes unresponsive while executions run
Webhook response time is affected by heavy production executions
doNotSkip: Do not confuse bad workflow design with platform scaling need
Do not ignore database storage growth

stages.label: managed PostgreSQL/Redis-ready observability
stages.architecture: single or larger n8n instance with managed database readiness, centralized logs, metrics, and alerts
entryCriteria: Traffic is steady enough to justify operations investment
Scaling decisions need reliable metrics
Team needs repeatable recovery and alerting
requiredSettings: managed PostgreSQL or equivalent DB runbook
alerts for p95 latency, failed executions, DB connections, memory, storage
exitSignals: Metrics show sustained execution concurrency pressure
Worker separation is needed to protect UI/API
Webhook ingress and execution workloads should be separated
doNotSkip: Do not add queue workers without seeing their logs
Do not run Redis as an unmonitored side process

stages.label: Redis queue mode + workers
stages.architecture: main n8n process, Redis message broker, PostgreSQL, one or more worker processes
entryCriteria: Production executions overlap enough to justify worker pool
Main UI/API or webhook coordination is affected by execution load
PostgreSQL and Redis are reachable and observable
requiredSettings: shared N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY across main and workers
binary data mode database or S3
exitSignals: Webhook ingress becomes bottleneck
Queue depth grows even after adding workers
Workers need separate pools by workload class
doNotSkip: Do not use filesystem binary data with queue mode
Do not set very low worker concurrency across many workers if DB connections are limited

stages.label: separate webhook processors + worker pools
stages.architecture: main process, webhook processor pool behind load balancer, Redis, workers, PostgreSQL
entryCriteria: Webhook request volume is the ingress bottleneck
Main process should not receive heavy webhook load
Workers already process production executions
requiredSettings: load balancer routes /webhook/* and /webhook-waiting/* to webhook processors
exitSignals: Need high availability for main process
Need independent rolling deploys
Need worker autoscaling and platform-level orchestration
doNotSkip: Do not add main process to the webhook load balancer pool
Do not route manual webhook-test traffic away from main

stages.label: managed DB/Redis + centralized logs + multi-main review
stages.architecture: queue mode with managed PostgreSQL, managed Redis, centralized logs and metrics, optional multi-main evaluation
entryCriteria: Queue mode is the core production path
Redis and database availability affect business operations
Team needs operational governance and HA evaluation
requiredSettings: managed PostgreSQL or mature self-managed equivalent
managed Redis or mature self-managed equivalent
centralized logs for main, webhook processors, workers, Redis, PostgreSQL, load balancer
all main and worker processes run same n8n version
sticky sessions if multi-main is used
exitSignals: Need pod scheduling or worker autoscaling
Need rolling deploy primitives across many processes
Need standard Kubernetes platform governance
doNotSkip: Do not enable multi-main without understanding leader/follower behavior
Do not scale worker count beyond DB connection capacity

stages.label: Kubernetes / OpenShift / EKS style orchestration
stages.architecture: main pods, worker pods, webhook processor pods, PostgreSQL, Redis, object storage, ingress, secrets, centralized logs
entryCriteria: Queue mode operations are already understood
DB/Redis/object storage are managed or professionally operated
Team needs rolling updates, worker autoscaling, pod scheduling, and platform governance
Team can operate Kubernetes incidents
exitSignals: Move to enterprise HA/multi-main architecture
Formal SRE ownership
Multi-environment platform lifecycle
doNotSkip: Do not start with Kubernetes to avoid learning queue mode
Do not hide DB/Redis/logging gaps behind pods
